speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4 with Mr. Sam Mathis, in Los Angeles, California, United States of America, on February the 20th, 1996. Mr. Mathis, you were telling us that the Russians gave you some time--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, you know, [INAUDIBLE] got killed. [INAUDIBLE], but I didn't do this. Uh-uh, no. [INAUDIBLE] free, condemned. Then they-- I told you-- they [INAUDIBLE]. I said, we're done. Because I was mad what they done to my-- my-- my sister. And I cannot stand it. After they taken my sister, they [INAUDIBLE]. My mother, she cry so much before they took her away. I can never forget it. Then going in city-- going-- going-- I'm going with my friend, not by myself. You never go by yourself. You never can go out, even after the war. You have to be careful. I'm going in there in the city with my friend, and I talk in-- to the German inside. And I see him [INAUDIBLE]. He took out a gun, run from the German. I went to out to the Russian. I said this man [INAUDIBLE] ammunition. He took him out. He killed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes! He kill him right away, the soldier, because he'll want to kill us. I run away. Again, I said to fight him [INAUDIBLE] outside [INAUDIBLE], on the outside inside [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] don't go inside. [INAUDIBLE] The Russian [INAUDIBLE] soldiers. [INAUDIBLE] the war. Now you have to be-- keep your mouth shut like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Russians kill a lot of Germans after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I don't know because I don't pay attention to this. I don't want to [INAUDIBLE]. It's their war. Not my war. My war was already done before, a long time ago. That's [INAUDIBLE]. This is what--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you came to the American zone, and the Americans didn't want to let you in.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. [INAUDIBLE]. Somebody from the train goes down. He took some pair of shoes for the [INAUDIBLE], the Germans, something. I don't know what. They lost medicine, going to send it back to-- to Czechoslovakia, Theresienstadt. They took us not to Theresienstadt because they'll have to take us to Salzburg. They took us [INAUDIBLE], Landsberg. OK. Landsberg. And then over there, I was [INAUDIBLE] from 1945, about [INAUDIBLE], maybe three months in-- after. Because I-- I was in Czechoslovakia. [INAUDIBLE] in block six, I think. And then I was in Landsberg. And Landsberg was-- you can never go out. [? Kill ?] [? for your ?] time. Because-- I was afraid because it was typhus, typhus. The Germans was afraid they going to die. They were-- they send-- they send Jewish [INAUDIBLE] life to death. They was afraid of the typhus. The Germans say the American wasn't [? them. ?] They said, no. You cannot go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: American-- American, [INAUDIBLE] something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camp. Yeah. In Landsberg [INAUDIBLE]. Then I find out-- now, after I go out and see what is in the camp, they're not going around. Walk around-- I walk around in-- in camp, walk around the hospital. [INAUDIBLE] hospital, I walk around, I see two women. They was with me in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they see me. Ah! I see the [INAUDIBLE]. You're alive! I happy to see them too. I said, you know where Dorin is-- Dorin is? Yeah. She-- I think she's around in another camp. I think she's in Feldafing something, [INAUDIBLE] another Jewish camp. Feldafing-- I like to see her. I like to see her. What can I do? I cannot go out from the camp. They said, you cannot go out. You know, violent. I don't know where [INAUDIBLE]. You have-- you cannot go out because they're afraid of typhus from the camps, but I [INAUDIBLE]. Now, I said to my friend. They said, Sam, [INAUDIBLE]. We call them anyway, but they won't. Don't go off now. You have-- you just liberated. Why you going looking for Dorin? [INAUDIBLE] longer. I said, no. I have to see if she's alive. I took in the night. I went out to the [INAUDIBLE]. I went out over there into the city, and I hitchhike with a truck from the Germans to her camp. I went to the camp, and got-- and I see people-- they know me from the other camps [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, you know what? Go tell her. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody, but they know-- [INAUDIBLE] know me. Not [INAUDIBLE]. Someone that know me. Somebody who knows me. You want to tell her about her-- about her boyfriend because she knows him. I don't tell him [INAUDIBLE] a surprise. Then she went over there, and said Dorin, somebody comes, and they want to tell you that they-- [INAUDIBLE] is still alive. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: She can see, and she fainted. After she see me, she fall-- she fall, faint. She never believe it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I said, you know what? I went with her. A few-- a few days. I was sleeping in a different barrack. She [INAUDIBLE] me the children, the children block. She was not young-- the children block. I said, I'm going to take you to my camp. OK. This is not over. My camp, you know, the American will get you in trouble too. My camp-- I got-- I got a piece of paper that was mine in Landsberg. She was living in children's camp. She'll have nothing. I said, what you gonna do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] run off from your camp. I took a boat from-- from Feldafing [? with ?] Dorin to the-- to my-- [INAUDIBLE] in Landsberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I said on way? [INAUDIBLE]. The American people, American soldiers, catch us and take us into [INAUDIBLE]. They said they'd send us back to Poland. They going to take us and send us-- I said, [INAUDIBLE]. I cannot talk English. And I said, there's not a Jew over there? I talk to Jewish. They said, there's a Jewish too. I said what? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I wasn't looking for my-- my girlfriend. Is she alive? And you gonna send me back to the Germans in Poland? For what the hell you gonna do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yes. We gonna send you back to Poland. We're gonna send you in a Polish camp in Poland. You go back to Poland. See, the American people, they're not too good. I telling you. The soldiers, not [INAUDIBLE] to the Jews. Maybe to somebody else, they treat different. I said this, and I started to cry. I said, I'll show you my passport, my [INAUDIBLE] from Landsberg. I take a [INAUDIBLE]. I find that she is here.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then they took us to Polish concentration camp in Poland. In Poland-- in Poland was better than the American-- the Polish-- Polish people-- Polish. They said, you're Jewish. Why you want say go back to Poland? Run out in the night! [INAUDIBLE] Go run out! [INAUDIBLE] Run [INAUDIBLE]. They're gonna take you to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell us. They give us courage. We run out in the night. We run out in the night from camp [INAUDIBLE] back [INAUDIBLE] and back to Landsberg. See, the Americans were trouble. There was trouble, American people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this camp where they told you they were going to send you back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send-- they sending them in-- over there, in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they send you back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Polish people-- yeah. They send me to-- they send me camp. All the Polish people that was in camp, they told me to run away, not to go back to Poland. They help me out. They said to give me courage. Don't do it. You're gonna [INAUDIBLE] to Poland. [INAUDIBLE] take a transfer to [INAUDIBLE] to Poland. What you gonna do? I said, come on. I said [INAUDIBLE], come on. Out. What it's gonna be now, it's gonna be. And they right. We run out in the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out during the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did, the Polish let me out. They let me out because they [INAUDIBLE] me out. They said, go out. They let me out, see? After all, they let me out because they was Polish prisoners from before, maybe. They let us out. They said, go. Then we [INAUDIBLE], and I took her to Landsberg. It's what happened. It's not [INAUDIBLE]. All the time [INAUDIBLE] Americans, [INAUDIBLE] Jew. It's everybody. That's what happened. Then she was over there. And then we married. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I got the [INAUDIBLE]. I never remember when. [LAUGHS] I remember. Yeah. Marriage is for 50 years-- November. November the 3rd. 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Landsberg am Lech?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg am Lech, not very long. [INAUDIBLE], oh, yeah. It was 1949. [INAUDIBLE]. 1949, I come to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find work. Fine-- [INAUDIBLE] in a-- in a [INAUDIBLE] tailor, a tailor shop for the government, for them to send-- see, [INAUDIBLE] I want to get the social security from them. They make me trouble a little bit. You know why? The reason why-- because when I was in the camp, then I-- I change my age. I make it younger. They can-- when I was in the camp after the war, [INAUDIBLE] because I don't want to go to Poland. I don't-- every country, not back to Poland. I was registered as [INAUDIBLE] every place, because I think I don't go back to Poland. I've got no [INAUDIBLE] left over in Poland-- to make a new life, a better, another country. Not in a country that helped the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I make it younger, right after the war-- it was-- I made it younger. Then when I come to the United States-- I come to the United States. I said I'm so younger. [INAUDIBLE] Why don't I go back to my age? Because started a war in-- in Korea. I was in Centralia. Centralia, the Jews was terrible. They sent-- they sent me to Centralia. You know, there was 50 families Jews, all the people, from-- from long, long time people. They said, you know what? Come and register to the war. I said, why the hell you want me to the war? I just come back from a war. A Jewish man-- his name was-- oh, my, my. [INAUDIBLE]. His name [INAUDIBLE], a Jewish man, a rich man. He had all so many gas stations. He said, come and register to war, Korea.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I've come-- I'm not a citizen even. I just come over! We're already to the war. I got enough of so many years, War. I said, well, before I get this-- I got-- first of all, I get over there. They don't got a place for me. When I come to the United States, they don't got a place-- they told me the Jewish Federation automatically-- I come here because I was a tailor. Over there, [INAUDIBLE]. When I was in Germany liberated, I went to school for the-- for designer. I get my diploma. I got my diploma here. [INAUDIBLE] Then I registered in America. And now [INAUDIBLE] said to me, Sam [? Mathis, ?] go register. You go [INAUDIBLE]. You gonna make it better than I did. I went and registered. And [INAUDIBLE] registered, and they ask me questions about tailor and everything, how they cut suit. I know everything. He said, you're so young. But that's OK. I know. And they decided after a few weeks, they-- they [? gave ?] me-- they sent me over to the Consulate. I never [INAUDIBLE] the Consulate because they said the Consulate so terrible, making the map. They show this little state and this state and this state and that state. [INAUDIBLE] you go to the Consulate. If-- if you're sick, you cannot go. They lay you off, and I was afraid with a child already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a child while you were in Landsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born 1946, a year-- after the war, a year later. 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- [INAUDIBLE] I forgot [? that ?] [? I talk. ?] You were telling us how you got to the United States and to Centralia, Illinois.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK. The-- the United States, they asked me if I got relatives. I said, I got nobody. I got nobody relatives. I never know even there are relatives. They said [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: In United States, I don't know, the Jewish family decided they want to get a family from-- from Europe to Centralia. Nobody [? lives ?] in Centralia. [INAUDIBLE]. What I do to there? I don't know what to do [? in ?] there. They don't got no place for me to go. Everybody-- a lot of my friends was in St. Louis, Missouri, Chicago, other places-- not in Centralia. Centralia's a small town, 50 families of Jews. Why the hell they need me there? [? For a sucker? ?] They don't need me over there! They don't got a place to give me to work. No place. They said-- The Jewish Federation sent-- they give me-- they-- first time, they give me $30 a month for everything for child, for rent, all together. I said after nine month, I decided I don't want them no more. I don't want anymore, because they used to come. They open my ice box, looking in-- why-- where you got this? Where you got the milk? Where you got the [? fleisch? ?] I said, what the hell? I come to the Untied States and suffer like this again? I said to Jewish Federation, I don't want you to come no more. They come from St. Louis. I don't want to see you no more. I'm going to work by myself, my own. But I'm gonna do what I'm gonna do. I don't want you. I send them out. I said, if you come, I'll kick you out. I don't want you no more. If you can look-- [INAUDIBLE]. I lost [INAUDIBLE] was in a temple. [INAUDIBLE] to live. And in temple, he came and looks, open the [INAUDIBLE] ice box. Where you got that meat? Where you got this? I said, by hell of this. I can't [INAUDIBLE]. In a place like this, and then [INAUDIBLE] make me trouble like this? I don't want to see you no more. Then I started to work. I said, don't [? got ?] no job for me. I don't care. They want to give me a tailor, nice store. They want me to pay a dollar, for-- pay [INAUDIBLE] come over here. I don't want to see you. I want to work for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, I can't work for myself. I work in-- in Landsberg for myself, but only [INAUDIBLE] for something [INAUDIBLE]. I started to work for myself, even I cannot talk. I told you [INAUDIBLE]. They say this is [INAUDIBLE] shorter this, longer this. I can't talk. I got good rent. [INAUDIBLE], I tell you, mouth cannot talk, but my hands can work. I know what to do. That's what I done. I started to do for-- oh, so many people. I start to do alterations. [INAUDIBLE] single, [INAUDIBLE] new suits. People used to come from all the cities. They said to him, send Mathis-- and-- I got even the-- the name [INAUDIBLE], Sam the tailor, the best tailor. I didn't need no more the Jews. I said, enough for me. I don't need it. And after a long year working, I bought me a car. The Jews that [INAUDIBLE], the same Jew, I bought the car-- I payed him $1,000 for a car. He charged me too much. [INAUDIBLE] remember. I don't care. He trying to teach the car-- to drive the car, how to drive a car. He teach me [? hard. ?] I decide to drive away. He said, don't drive here! [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask him, what you want me to do? To drive in the house? I can drive in the streets. I cannot drive in the house the car. I said, I have to drive it. I said-- he said, give me the car. I said, oh, you not getting out the car. [INAUDIBLE]. I'm gonna drive the car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS] I drive the car. Then I-- then I took the car in the morning for myself. I pay him $1,000. [INAUDIBLE] And tried to back in and back out. Yes. I did it myself. And I got customers, and I got a very nice man. He was [? shoots ?] police-- no, no, a state police. [INAUDIBLE] The lead of the state police, he been customer. And I talked to him. I need license. He [INAUDIBLE]. He said, I give you the license. He send me the license, and I was a good driver. [LAUGHS] For so many years, I drive. [INAUDIBLE] and see how many years I drive it here. So many years. He helped me out. I have the best customers over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, the same Jews, he came to me to work. [INAUDIBLE] do my this, do my this. I said, why I have to do this and do this for what? You do not help me out. You look in the mirror. You look in this. The-- the [INAUDIBLE]. I used to talk Yiddish a little bit. Before I started talk English, I said, they joke [? with us. ?] I talk and I did. I said, you can joke. Then this, how can you drive a car? When you drive a car, you-- you go all over these people. It's gonna kill people. You know what I used to do? I used to drive the car to the temple on Sundays. I used to go to Sunday school. I used to blow my horn and show. I don't-- I don't drive on the [INAUDIBLE]. I drive on the streets. You-- your sons don't know how to drive. I know. I [INAUDIBLE]. I used to do him special like this. Special. Ask my wife. It's the truth. I used to do it special to show them. I-- You know what happened? I've working so many years [INAUDIBLE], Centralia, the Chamber of Commerce, they don't like for me to move away from Centralia. You'll never believe what they said. They said to me, they would like a lot of [INAUDIBLE] out. Not you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to get customers come into the city from all around over there-- customers. They used to have more people. But I, after two years, I bought my home. I bought a home over there. And after a few years later, I bought-- I make addition to the home. I can show you the [INAUDIBLE]. I got the this little  picture-- big picture. Addition to this, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I sold it. I never got in [INAUDIBLE] from the Jews, I never let them. When I run away from the city, they said, you gonna go from a small town like a big city like Los Angeles and ocean? I said, don't worry for me. Don't worry for me. I will go there. Because people living over there, [INAUDIBLE]. You know? You used to [? be in the ?] [? army ?] in Los Angeles. [INAUDIBLE] He can go. He can go to Los Angeles. You not afraid-- not afraid? I said, I'm not afraid. I have four children. With four children, with a car traveling there. [INAUDIBLE] talk, traveling, coming with nobody. With nobody. My wife bought a [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] said, you go back. I'm going on to see [INAUDIBLE], where I'm gonna look for a job. But I'm not going looking for a job. Gonna see what is going on here. I did it for myself with my wife. And so you came to the United States with one daughter and how many other children did you have in Illinois?

speaker: interviewee
text: Illinois, three more. Got one-- got [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is Joanne. Joanne was born in-- Joanne was born in Centralia-- yeah. Centralia, yeah. Joanne, Hanna, and Renee. And Tina's born here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you one question. When you first came to the United States, did anyone ever encourage you to talk about your experiences? Were they interested in hearing what happened? Nothing. Listen, if you want to ask me another question, I'm gonna ask you another-- I'll tell you something else. You remind me a little more about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: To get the citizen papers for me was so hard in Centralia. They promise me they gonna send me to school. They never sent us to school. They never do nothing. They-- when I want to go [INAUDIBLE] citizen, I ask him to teach us something. They [INAUDIBLE]. I took a book-- both myself and my wife-- [INAUDIBLE]. And I show you a paper. I got here the paper to show you for the-- for the federal judge, [INAUDIBLE]. He was my customer. He had a speech about me. He said, the best citizen-- he said Mathis, you're the best tailor in our city. Yes. I show him-- the newspaper. I got pictures. I forgot to show you. You asked me about pictures. I'll show you pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: You come to United States with your brothers? Yes. They don't want to help us, nothing, the Jews. No. My wife said, don't tell them. They don't like-- they-- she tell me not to tell you because you don't like it. I tell the truth. This happened like this. 'Cause this-- 'cause this-- maybe this was younger people. [INAUDIBLE]. All the people, why they need us for suffer-- to suffer over there again? After 11 years, we suffered 11 years for nothing over there. [INAUDIBLE]. The children was older, was going around the-- in the night. Sometimes in the night, you know what I do to the neighbor over there? They knock on my door. They-- they call me up over there, the-- the Gentiles in Centralia-- you know what they do? Some time ago, they sent-- they send me the driver's license-- somebody stole my driver's licenses in the mail. Then the police calls me up. I have made accident, took my license. What you mean my license? My car's in-- in the garage.

speaker: interviewee
text: I call up my-- my-- the dealer about the car. I said, you told me it takes longer time. My license is already somebody sent. They drive with my name, then they bring back the license. It was trouble. That's why I left the city. I said, I cannot stay no more. If not there, I would go to St. Louis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you come to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I-- no-- I was-- some time I was over there, [INAUDIBLE] in an accident [INAUDIBLE] my home and was working around the house. Fell down, my back was starting to hurt. I got down in the night from the bed, I said--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a customer, [INAUDIBLE]. He was a eye doctor, good doctor from Germany, a Jew. He said, Sam, if you want to go for you, are you still making new suits? You still go before me. He used to go to St. Louis. He never went to St. Louis no more. I show a letter. Then, I said to him-- he said to me, don't go. Don't go no place. You go to Los Angeles. You're gonna make it over there. You go do it. And that is that. When did you come to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked as a tailor here also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My shop was in-- [INAUDIBLE] in Brentwood, on Sunset and [INAUDIBLE] Street. I'll show you-- movie stars were my customers. All of them. No advertisers. They advertise me when you go out to the dinner or something. Said, where you make it? Go to Samson-- my [INAUDIBLE] Samson [? the ?] [? tailor ?]. Samson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tina-- I say Tina, but actually, [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] Amanda and Raquel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now that it's more than 50 years after the war, I'd like to ask you, is there any message that you would like to give to your children, your grandchildren, or future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope this never happen again. That's it. Nothing better to tell. I hope it never happen again. But go [INAUDIBLE], with the skinheads, you never know. I hope it never happens again. I hope. Because I'm not gonna be alive any more anyway. I hope that never happen, never [INAUDIBLE] me again. [SOBBING] No. Bad life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even the best life here is no good. Something happened. Everything takes away for you. Everything, it's all your life. Evil. No good. [INAUDIBLE] You can-- you can't do nothing. You try. What can you do? [INAUDIBLE] can do nothing. The best you can, you do. Until now, you know, I don't think I can tell you this. [INAUDIBLE] my daughter, she calls me up, and said, Daddy, how you doing? My-- she had a friend over there. They did it. How come you don't do it? I said, [INAUDIBLE] you was home, I'm asking-- I want to write it. I tell you to write down everything. You was not interested. What you want me to do now? I decided OK. I'm going to do it. Thank you very much. I want to thank you for taking the time to give us your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad for the [INAUDIBLE]. I feel better a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to take a look at some of your family photos and meet your wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken Radzyn. In [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 15 years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Tell us who this is.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you were in Hashomer Hatzair?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 16 years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in Radzyn, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember very good. Maybe in the war or before the war. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think it was right around 1939?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and the man-- And the man on the right side is someone from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're the second one from the left side wearing the coat?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Radzyn. In Poland, in Radzyn, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after the liberation in Theresienstadt, Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberation. Yeah. Yes. After liberation in Theresienstadt, in Czechoslovakia. They make a transport to go in the American zone, because I don't want to go back to Poland. And I'm in the left side, the first one with the white band on my arm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Shulim Moshe. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in Landsberg am Lech in Deutschland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is you and your wife, Dorin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Landsberg am Lech, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was right after your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: 45. OK. This is my [? Frau, ?] Dorin, and my name Shulim Moshe. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Scheindel, in Landsberg am Lech in Deutschland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is you and your wife, Dorin, and your first daughter Sharon--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us who's in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [INAUDIBLE] family. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in Los Angeles in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us the names of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the kinder is-- is Sharon, Tina, Hanah Esther, and Renee. And their [GERMAN] is Steve, Dudley, and Ron.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our grandchildren. One is-- is-- is Amanda. This is Tina's daughter, our grandchild. And the bottom is Raquel. This is Renee's daughter. This is two [INAUDIBLE] grandchildren. Hanna's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in the picture.
